title: Field Briefing: Semantics as Shared Infrastructure
summary: Field Briefing: Semantics as Shared Infrastructure retrieves prior HTML skills in an original cross-device meaning briefing scenario and requires source, parsed-structure, keyboard, and content evidence.
objectives: Apply semantic landmarks in unfamiliar content.
Produce or audit a real cross-device meaning briefing artifact without appearance-only reasoning.
Keep earlier document requirements active while verifying native behavior and valid relationships.

content.text: Choose the claim that preserves content meaning when visual styling and browser recovery are removed.

options.text: Cross-device meaning briefing case 1: use main for every visually large panel so assistive technology finds all important boxes.
Cross-device meaning briefing case 1: choose each region from its content relationship and navigation purpose, then verify the resulting landmark map.
Cross-device meaning briefing case 1: wrap every paragraph in nav because users may want to reach it.

steps.title: Read the cross-device meaning briefing contract · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 4: Which practice follows the explanation when styling changes? Scenario: cross-device meaning briefing.
steps.why: Field Briefing: Semantics as Shared Infrastructure keeps meaning testable instead of depending on appearance. This is checkpoint 4 in cross-device meaning briefing.

content.text: Landmark elements identify major page regions by purpose. Header introduces a page or section; nav groups major navigation; main contains the unique primary content; aside is complementary; article can stand independently; section groups a named theme; and footer carries contextual closing information. A page normally exposes one main landmark and gives repeated navigation regions distinct names. Apply it to cross-device meaning briefing.

Cross-device meaning briefing case 4: header, section, article, aside, and div are interchangeable boxes once CSS makes them look identical.
